coredata.dc:title: Construction of the biomass carbon quantum dots modified heterojunction Bi2WO6/Cu2O photocatalysis for enhancing light utilization and mechanism insight
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Yang
Zhu, Zhi
Liu, Zhi
Dong, Hongjun
Liu, Yang
Wei, Maobin
Huo, Pengwei
Li, Chunxiang
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: In order to achieve the rational use of biomass resources and obtain efficient visible-light-responsive photocatalysts, the CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O is firstly synthesized, and exhibits excellent degradation activity under visible light for tetracycline (TC) degradation. The excellent photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O is due to the heterojunction of Bi2WO6/Cu2O which can effectively promote the separation of electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, the up-conversion effect of CQDs can enrich electrons and orient conductive electrons. Moreover, the possible photocatalytic mechanisms including transfer behaviors of charge carriers, generation reactive species, degrading intermediate products of TC are revealed in depth. This work may provide a new strategy for designing high stability and strong visible-light-response photocatalysts utilizing earth-abundant and low-cost natural materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enriched electrons
Biomass carbon quantum dots
Electron transfer
Up-conversion effect
Degradation of tetracycline

BK 20181231 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (no. 2015M571683 , 1501102B , 2016M590418 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of prepared materials: (a) Bi2WO6, Cu2O, CQDs and standard XRD patterns for Bi2WO6 and Cu2O, (b) CQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O composites.
TEM images of pure (a) Bi2WO6, (b) CQDs, (c) pure Cu2O, (d, e) CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O, (f, g) corresponding HR-TEM image of CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O.

CQDs (a) Up-converted PL spectra, (b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Photocatalytic activities degradation TC of as prepared samples under visible light illumination and (b) trapping experiment of active species using IPA, TEOA and VC.
The mechanism for degradation of TC under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Construction of the biomass carbon quantum dots modified heterojunction Bi2WO6/Cu2O photocatalysis for enhancing light utilization and mechanism insight

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of the Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of the Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aInstitute of the Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of physics, Jilin Normal University, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCollege of physics, Jilin Normal University, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to achieve the rational use of biomass resources and obtain efficient visible-light-responsive photocatalysts, the CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O is firstly synthesized, and exhibits excellent degradation activity under visible light for tetracycline (TC) degradation. The excellent photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O is due to the heterojunction of Bi2WO6/Cu2O which can effectively promote the separation of electron-hole pairs. Meanwhile, the up-conversion effect of CQDs can enrich electrons and orient conductive electrons. Moreover, the possible photocatalytic mechanisms including transfer behaviors of charge carriers, generation reactive species, degrading intermediate products of TC are revealed in depth. This work may provide a new strategy for designing high stability and strong visible-light-response photocatalysts utilizing earth-abundant and low-cost natural materials.

ce:section.ce:para: The typical Aurivillius-related oxide n-Bi2WO6 semiconductor due to its low cost, non-toxicity and high stability have aroused great attention, and the photo-generated electrons and holes pairs can be separated by the layered structure [1]. However, the fast recombination rate of photo-generated carriers has hindered the practical application of Bi2WO6. To date, many attempts have been made to form semiconductor heterostructure to hinder the recombination rates of photoinduced electrons-holes pairs by coupling with other charge acceptor, such as a matched band structure semiconductor [2–5].

It is found that Cu2O is a typical p-type metal oxide semiconductor with a narrow band gap that can enhance the photocatalytic activity. It has a large absorption solar spectrum, making it suitable for conversion of solar to chemical energy [6–8]. Generally, it is believed that the binary Bi2WO6/Cu2O heterostructure can effectively enhance the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. However, the electrons transport between simple composites is not sufficient and a part of electrons return to the photocatalyst, resulting in a decrease in photocatalytic activity. Related studies have been reported to overcome the above shortcomings [9–12] and proved that the combination of carbon material could enhance the photocatalytic degradation rates by increasing electron transfer efficiency and inhibiting the recombination of electrons and holes [13,14].

Biomass carbon is a novel carbon material, on the one hand, biomass source is a renewable raw material widely distributed in our country [15–17]. On the other hand, biomass carbon has good stability and electrons transport performance. However, the seldom-reported biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derive from biomass carbon. In general, CQDs, as a novel and environmentally friendly nano-carbon materials, whose size is 2–10 nm typically in diameter, consists of a relatively new class of zero-dimensional (0-D) nanostructures. Meanwhile, it has attracted a great deal of attention for its applications [18]. In fact, CQDs not only have similar excellent optical characteristics, but also own good biocompatibility, and excellent fluorescence stability. Therefore, CQDs is successfully combined with n-type semiconductor Bi2WO6 and p-type semiconductor Cu2O, the photocatalytic performance is greatly improved contributed to up-conversion properties effective harvesting of solar energy and construction of heterojunction. CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O system successful synthesis greatly overcomes the mentioned above shortcomings.

In the work, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O is evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of TC. Additionally, we further insightfully investigated the degradation mechanism of CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O, through the morphology, structure, photoelectric properties and the possible photogenerated carried migration process.

Na2WO4·2H2O (AR), Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (AR), H2SO4 (98%), CH3COOH(AR), NaOH (AR), CuCl2 (AR), glucose (AR), tetracycline (TC), isopropanol (IPA, AR), triethanolamine (TEOA, AR), ascorbic acid (VC, AR), ethanol (C2H5OH, AR) and 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co Ltd. All reagents were used as received from commercial suppliers without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout all experiments.
The synthesis process, photocatalytic activity test, the photo-electrochemical measurements and characterization are given in the supporting information.

The crystalline structures of the as-prepared samples are investigated by XRD analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the XRD pattern displays a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 22° which matches well with CQDs [19]. For pure Bi2WO6, the distinct diffraction peaks located at 28.5°, 31.8°, 47.1°, 56.0° and 58.5°corresponding to the (1 3 1), (2 0 0), (2 0 2), (1 3 3) and (2 6 2) crystal planes of orthorhombic Bi2WO6, which is in good agreement with the reported data (JCPDS card No. 39–0256) [20]. Moreover, for the Cu2O diffraction peaks can be easily indexed to a pure cubic phase of cuprous oxide (JCPDS card No. 05–0667) [21]. In addition, Fig. 1b shows the XRD patterns for the CQDs/Bi2WO6 composites, the characteristic diffraction peaks of CQDs is found, which indicates the formation of CQDs/Bi2WO6 composites. The typical diffraction peaks corresponding to the CQDs and Cu2O appear in the CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O composites and no peaks of other impurities are observed, indicating that the CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O photocatalyst is successfully prepared.

The microstructures of the samples are characterized by TEM and HR-TEM. The Bi2WO6 (Fig. 2 a) shows a 3D hierarchical sphere structures, which is consistent with the results of the SEM. From the TEM image of Fig. 2b, we can find the CQDs size with 2–6 nm. Moreover, the morphology of the Cu2O shows different sizes and irregular cubic structure (Fig. 2c). 3D hierarchical sphere structures of Bi2WO6/Cu2O and irregular sized cube structures Cu2O can be clearly seen from Fig. 2d. In the magnified TEM image of CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O in Fig. 2e, CQDs are well-dispersed on the surface and a large number of CQDs are evenly grown to the surface of Bi2WO6 flowers-like. In Fig. 2(f, g) the lattice fringe spacing is 0.268 nm and 0.32 nm corresponding to Bi2WO6 and Cu2O, respectively. Based on above description and discussion, CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O composite is successfully synthesized. To further explore the elemental composition of the sample, the elemental mapping patterns results are shown in Fig. S1.

The as-prepared CQDs are displayed to possess clear up-conversion PL properties as shown in Fig. 3 a. Since CQDs is excited by long-wavelength light, from 650 to 900 nm, the up-conversion emission is significantly in the range of 400 to 600 nm. The above results indicate CQDs by converting the near-infrared emission wavelength into visible light, which improves the photocatalytic efficiency. As shown in Fig. 3b, the maximum emission intensity is centered at 440 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The as-prepared CQDs are faint yellow under daylight and emit a brighter blue fluorescence under the excitation of 360 nm UV light. Moreover, the CQDs exhibits excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior.

Photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples are studied by degradation of TC solution (20 mg−1) under the visible light. Fig. 4 (a) shows the photocatalytic ability of the prepared samples. The CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O has the highest photocatalytic performance than Bi2WO6, Cu2O, CQDs/Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/Cu2O. The photocatalytic degradation rate can reached to 80% in 90 min, which further demonstrates that the influences CQDs leads to efficient separation of photo-excited electron-hole pairs, which consequently may be beneficial to photocatalytic performance. Since CQDs is excited by long-wavelength light, up-conversion properties could transfer low energy photons to high energy photons, resulting in enhanced visible light response and it implies that introduction of CQDs could effectively improve degradation performance of CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O composites. The trapping agent VC is utilized for quenching the superoxide radical (·O2 −) and IPA is used for hydroxyl radicals (·OH) while TEOA is used for holes (h+) during CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O degradation of TC. It greatly inhibits the photocatalytic degradation of TC after adding the IPA, TEOA and VC (Fig. 4b). The results indicate the ·OH, h+, and ·O2 − are all active species and play the key roles in photocatalytic degradation of TC.

The enhanced photocatalytic activity can be explained as follows: firstly, CQDs possess obvious up-conversion properties that can absorb near-infrared light and transfer low energy photons to high energy photons resulting in enhanced visible light response and utilization. The effect of up-conversion performance causes Bi2WO6 and Cu2O to form more electron-hole pairs. Therefore, semiconductors material can effectively absorb the entire spectrum. Secondly, CQDs can act as an electronic acceptor that directs the transfer of electrons where enriched in the conduction band (CB) of Cu2O particles, thereby inhibiting the electron-hole pairs recombination probability. Thirdly, the formation of Z-heterojunction Bi2WO6/Cu2O can accelerate the separation of electron-hole pairs at the surface of Bi2WO6 and Cu2O, and the recombination of the photo-generated charge carriers will be greatly reduced.

Based on the above analysis, the possible photocatalytic mechanism scheme is discussed and explained the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O composites. According to the above experiments, ·O2 −, ·OH and h+ all are involved in photocatalytic degradation. As shown in Fig. 5 , firstly, when CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O is exposed by visible-light, the Bi2WO6 and Cu2O produced electrons-hole pairs, the photo-generated electrons in the CB of Bi2WO6 tend to transfer and recombine with the photo-generated holes in the VB of Cu2O. Secondly, the more photo-generated electrons accumulated in the CB of Cu2O and CQDs can act as an electronic acceptor. Electrons can further react with O2 to form more ·O2 − or react with TC and the photo-generated holes in the VB of Bi2WO6 react with H2O/OH− to form OH [22,23], respectively. Therefore, the possible photocatalytic reactions are shown in Fig. 5. Based on the above analysis, the charge carrier transfers and degradation reactions are proposed as follows:
(1)

+ TC
→ OX
. Prod
.

In summary, a novel CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O composite photocatalyst is successfully synthesized by a facile hydrothermal process. The as-prepared CQDs/Bi2WO6/Cu2O exhibits better photocatalytic activity (degradation rate is 80%) than that of pure Bi2WO6 and Cu2O. Moreover, the up-conversion of CQDs derived peach blossom can enhance the light absorption and Z-heterojunction promotes transfer and separation of the photo-generated electrons and holes. This work once again represents a significant step for developing high-efficiency heterostructure photocatalytic materials and modified by low-cost biomass carbon rich in nature.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. U1662125, U151026, 21546013, and 21676127), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (no. BK 20181231), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (no. 2015M571683, 1501102B, 2016M590418).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2019.05.029.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doping effect of phosphate in Bi2WO6 and universal improved photocatalytic activity for removing various pollutants in water
Appl Catal B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Li, G. Chen, J. Sun, J. Rao, Z. Han, Y. Hu, W. Xing, C. Zhang, Doping effect of phosphate in Bi2WO6 and universal improved photocatalytic activity for removing various pollutants in water, Appl Catal, B, 188 (2016) 39–47.
